content: Always warm up for 5-10 minutes before exercising. Light cardio like jumping jacks, jogging in place, or arm circles prepare your muscles and reduce injury risk. Dynamic stretches are better than static before workouts.
Cool down for 5-10 minutes after each workout. Static stretching, slow walking, and deep breathing help recovery. Hold each stretch for 15-30 seconds without bouncing.

Progressive overload means gradually increasing weight, reps, or intensity over time. This is the key to muscle growth and strength gains. Increase by 5-10% when current weight becomes easy for all sets.

Rest days are essential for muscle recovery and growth. Beginners should take 2-3 rest days per week. Never train the same muscle group on consecutive days. Active recovery like walking or yoga is fine on rest days.
Compound exercises work multiple muscles simultaneously. They are more efficient than isolation exercises. Key compound movements: squats, deadlifts, bench press, overhead press, rows, and pull-ups.

For muscle gain, aim for 1.6-2.2g protein per kg bodyweight daily. Good sources: chicken breast (31g/100g), eggs (6g each), paneer (18g/100g), dal (9g/100g), whey protein (24g/scoop). Spread protein across meals.
Drink at least 3-4 liters of water daily. During workouts, drink 200-300ml every 15-20 minutes. Dehydration reduces performance by up to 25%. Urine should be light yellow - darker means drink more.
Sleep 7-9 hours per night for optimal recovery. Growth hormone is released during deep sleep. Poor sleep increases cortisol (stress hormone) which promotes fat storage and muscle breakdown.

Weight loss requires calorie deficit of 500 cal/day for 0.5kg/week loss. Combine cardio and strength training. Don't cut below 1200 cal (women) or 1500 cal (men). High protein helps preserve muscle during cuts.

Muscle gain requires calorie surplus of 300-500 cal/day. Focus on compound exercises. Train each muscle group 2x/week. Get adequate protein (1.6-2.2g/kg). Progressive overload is key. Be patient - 0.5-1kg muscle/month is good.
Effective home workouts are possible with no equipment. Use bodyweight exercises, household items as weights (water bottles, backpacks). Focus on higher reps and slower tempo to increase difficulty.

Flexibility training prevents injuries and improves performance. Do dynamic stretches before workouts, static stretches after. Yoga is excellent for overall flexibility. Hold stretches 15-30 seconds, don't bounce.

Indian diet for muscle gain: Breakfast - oats with milk and banana, 4 eggs. Lunch - 2 rotis, chicken/paneer, dal, rice, salad. Snack - sprouts, nuts, protein shake. Dinner - roti, chicken/tofu, vegetables. Includes 100-130g protein daily.

Indian diet for weight loss: Breakfast - poha/upma with vegetables. Lunch - 1 roti, dal, sabzi, small rice, curd. Snack - fruits, green tea, roasted chana. Dinner - soup, salad, grilled paneer/chicken. Total ~1500-1800 calories.

Basic supplements: Whey protein (if diet protein is low), creatine monohydrate (5g daily for strength), vitamin D3 (if deficient), omega-3 fish oil. Most supplements are unnecessary if diet is balanced.

Common gym mistakes: skipping warm-up, ego lifting (too heavy), poor form, no progressive overload, skipping leg day, too much cardio when building muscle, not tracking workouts, inconsistency, comparing to others.

LISS (Low Intensity Steady State): walking, cycling 30-60 min. Good for fat burning, recovery. HIIT (High Intensity Interval Training): 20-30 min alternating intense and rest periods. Burns more calories in less time.

Eat protein within 2 hours of training for recovery. Pre-workout meal 1-2 hours before: carbs + moderate protein. Post-workout: protein + carbs within 1 hour. Total daily intake matters more than exact timing.

To break plateaus: change rep ranges, try new exercises, increase intensity techniques (drop sets, supersets), deload for a week, ensure adequate sleep and nutrition, check if you're actually tracking progress.
Prevent injuries: always warm up, use proper form (ego check), don't increase weight too fast (max 10%/week), balance push/pull exercises, include mobility work, listen to your body, take rest days.
